title: Port Suez
subtitle: 6-May-41
text: The SS Dempo in Liverpool Dock, England, in March 1941, while being converted from a cruise liner into a troop carrier. Dave embarked on her at Durban, South Africa, on 21 April 1941, arriving at Port Suez on 6 May 1941.
caption: army cards

title: Cairo
subtitle: Sep-41
text: 8th Army headquarters was established in Cairo, and Dave was assigned to the Eighth Army- This is his uniform Clasp
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Alexandria
subtitle: Oct-41
text: During a short visit to Alexandria, before heading into battle Dave sent this photo to his mom. She noted on the back that she  "received it on 22/11/14 in Pretoria" South Africa
caption: Photo to Mom

title: El Alemein
subtitle: 18-Nov-41
text: The Operation Crusader uniform patches Dave wore during the build-up of supply lines and the winning of the first battle at El Alemain which was won by 25/11/1941
caption: Crusader Badge

title: Bir Hacheim
subtitle: 4-Feb-42
text: The 8th Army advanced and re-grouped here to rest and re-stock supplies
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Bir Hacheim
subtitle: 19-May-42
text: Daves letter posted home to his mom via APO (Army Post Office) on 19/5/1942
caption: letter to mom

title: Gazala
subtitle: 21-Jun-42
text: On 26 May 1942, the Battle of Gazala began. This time the Eighth Army was defeated. Tobruk fell, and 32,000 Allied troops were captured. The Eighth Army retreated eastward and by 30 June 1942, Rommel's Axis forces reached El Alamein again
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: El Alemein
text: Months later, on 23 October 1942, the Second Battle of El Alamein began — the battle that would change everything and last until 11Nov 1942. Under the new command of Lieutenant Montgomery, the Eighth Army launched a massive offensive and won. Here Gen Jan Smuts visits the SA troops in their distinguising "Tin Hats"
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: el-Hammah
subtitle: 20-Mar-43
text: On this date, Allied forces—primarily elements of the British Eighth Army—launched probing and preliminary attacks against Axis positions around El Hamma. This action was essentially a testing engagement, helping Montgomery assess the strength of the Mareth Line defences.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Wādī al-ʿAkārīt
subtitle: 6-Apr-43
text:
After retreating from the Battle of the Mareth Line, Axis forces made their stand here. At Wādī al-ʿAkārīt (often called the Battle of Akarit) Fighting was intense, especially on key ridges like Djebel Fatnassa.
Despite heavy resistance, Allied troops broke through the defensive line.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Enfidha
subtitle: 20-Apr-43
text:
This battle took place near the town of Enfidha. Despite determined attacks, Allied forces made limited progress and suffered significant casualties.
The battle became largely a stalemate, with heavy fighting continuing for several days.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Medjerda
subtitle: 6-May-43
text:
The fighting occurred along the Medjerda River valley. The Allied forces broke through Axis defenses along the Medjerda. This advance contributed directly to the fall of Tunis on 7 May 1943.
Within days, all Axis forces in North Africa surrendered (around 13 May 1943).
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Tunis

title: Port Suez
text: Dave embarked at Port Suez for Durban on The Home Exchange Program. During this leave he had his Photo taken at a Pretoria photographer - Wearing his Operation crusader uniform Badge
caption: Army cards

title: Port Suez
text: On 6 November 1943 Dave disembarked from SS Duchess of Richmond (from Durban) after Home Leave
caption: Army cards

title: Cairo
text: Transferred to Citizen Military to prepare to leave for Italy
caption: Army cards

title: Helwan
text: Admitted to Hospital No5 after Accidental Injury(Bike accident)
caption: Army cards

title: Alexandria
text: Discharged from Hospital to Base and left for Italy
caption: Army cards

title: Reggio de Calabria
subtitle: 1-Jun-44
text: 8th Army troops landed at this port from Alexandria, and so did Dave, but because he had been in Hospital in Cairo when his unit went to Italy, he caught up with his fellow soldiers for the first time in Sep 1944 near Rimini.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Coriano
text: The Battle of Coriano was a hard-fought engagement during the Allied advance through northern Italy, forming part of the larger offensive against the Gothic Line which had been defensively set up by the Germans where the area was sown with land mines, laced with barbed wire, and covered by carefully positioned artillery and machine-gun fire.
caption: 8th Army Clasp

title: Emelia Romano Region
text: The Allied Army Base was in the Emelia Romano Region where Allied soldiers are receiving Newspapers from home. Both side had to wait out the Italian winter between Oct 1944 and Mar 1945. This is the region Dave wanted to visit there in 1984
caption: Italy visit

title: Po River at Ferara
text: Dave kept this leaflet which was handed out to soldiers for encouragement to push accross the Po River. The Po River was the last major natural barrier in Italy. Allied engineers launched rapid assault crossings using boats and pontoon bridges.Crossing points were established near Ferrara and other sectors. Air superiority from the Allied Air Forces disrupted German withdrawal routes
caption: Prpaganda leaflet

title: Venice
text: Crossing the Po River marked the end of organized resistance in Italy. What followed was a swift collapse of the German defensive line. The Italian campaign became a swift, decisive sweep to the Alps, ending in total collapse.
caption: Post cards

title: Lignano Sabbia d'Ora
text: Eighth Army (east): Po River → Padua → Venice → northeast toward Trieste. meeting only minor skirmishs along the way
caption: Post cards

title: Udine
text: Benito Mussolini was executed by his fellow Italians on 28 April 1945 , and Adolf Hitler committed suicide on 30 April 1945
caption: Post cards

title: Trieste
text: Germany OUT!
caption: Newspaper

title: Cairo-Emplaned for Union
text: Demobilised - end of war
caption: Army cards

title: Nairobi
text: Plane in Nairobi to re-fuel on the way to South Africa
caption: newspaper
